---

## The Law of Large Numbers

The principle is the **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** — one of the foundational theorems of probability theory.

It states: as the number of independent, identically distributed random trials increases, the sample mean converges to the expected value (population mean). In formal terms:

> For i.i.d. random variables X₁, X₂, …, Xₙ with finite mean μ:
> P(|X̄ₙ − μ| > ε) → 0 as n → ∞, for any ε > 0

### Why it unifies all three systems

The key mechanism is **variance suppression through aggregation**. The standard deviation of the sample mean scales as σ/√n — so relative fluctuations shrink as sample size grows:

| System | Individual (unpredictable) | Aggregate (predictable) | What "n" is |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geiger counter | Single atom's decay timing | Decay rate over many atoms | Number of atoms in the sample |
| Insurance | Whether one person files a claim | Claim frequency across the portfolio | Number of policyholders |
| Statistical mechanics | One molecule's velocity | Pressure, temperature | ~10²³ molecules (Avogadro scale) |

### Why this is deeper than it appears

The critical insight — and what the Suppress signals correctly flag against analyzing in isolation — is that this is **not** about the individual events becoming more predictable. Each atom, each policyholder, each molecule remains fully random. The predictability is an **irreducibly collective property**: it exists only at the aggregate level and cannot be attributed to any individual component.

This is also why statistical mechanics works so spectacularly well: n ≈ 10²³ makes σ/√n effectively zero in relative terms, so macroscopic properties like pressure and temperature appear *deterministic* even though they emerge from chaos.

The closely related **Central Limit Theorem** complements the LLN by specifying *how* the aggregate fluctuates around the predictable mean — it follows a normal distribution regardless of the underlying individual distribution. This is why actuarial tables can compute precise confidence intervals, and why physicists can calculate exact fluctuation bounds for thermodynamic quantities.

**In one sentence:** Individual randomness washes out in aggregation because the Law of Large Numbers guarantees that the mean of many independent random outcomes converges to the true expected value, with deviations shrinking proportionally to 1/√n.
